---
title: "Paper 1: Adoption Patterns of Compliant Authoring Tools in UK Business Schools"
---

::: {.contribution}
I designed the study, drafted and piloted the survey instrument, recruited the participating institutions, collected and cleaned the data, performed the analysis, and wrote the manuscript. Co-author A advised on sampling strategy and reviewed successive drafts.
:::

## Abstract

Cross-sectional survey of doctoral programme directors at 28 UK business schools investigating patterns of adoption of policy-compliant authoring tools. We find substantial heterogeneity across institutions, with adoption rates ranging from 8% to 71%.

## Introduction

Policy-compliant authoring tools have proliferated rapidly over the past five years, but their uptake in doctoral education is poorly documented [@smith2024innovation].

## Methods

A web-administered survey was sent to doctoral programme directors at all 28 AACSB-accredited UK business schools. The response rate was 89% ($n = 25$ usable responses).

## Results

Adoption rates ranged from 8% to 71% with a mean of 34%. Two institutional factors are associated with higher adoption:

- **Institutional size**: a one-standard-deviation increase in cohort size is associated with a 6.2 percentage-point increase in adoption ($p < 0.05$).
- **Dedicated methods training**: programmes with a formal research-methods sequence show 11.4 percentage-point higher adoption ($p < 0.01$).

## Discussion

The findings suggest that adoption is structurally enabled by institutional resources rather than driven by individual candidate preferences.

## Limitations

The cross-sectional design precludes causal claims.

## Appendix 2.A: Survey instrument

The full 18-item survey instrument is reproduced below. Items were piloted with three programme directors and refined based on cognitive interviews.

::: {.callout-note}
## Section 1: Programme demographics

Items 1.1–1.4 capture institution size, accreditation status, and doctoral cohort size.
:::

## Appendix 2.B: Sampling frame

The sampling frame was constructed from the AACSB UK directory accessed in September 2025.
